event: During the African Humid Period, the Sahara region of present-day Niger is a green savanna with lakes, supporting hunter-gatherers and early herders depicted in the Aïr and Termit rock art.
Pottery and early cattle pastoralism develop in the Termit region, marking some of the earliest food-producing societies in the area.
Progressive desiccation of the Sahara begins, pushing populations southward toward the Niger River and Lake Chad basins.
Iron smelting appears in the Termit Massif, among the earliest evidence of independent ironworking in sub-Saharan Africa.
Trans-Saharan exchange networks and seasonal trade routes begin linking communities of the southern Sahara with North Africa.
Berber and Tuareg pastoralists expand into the Aïr Mountains, while Hausa communities coalesce in the southern lands.
The Kanem Empire rises east around Lake Chad, extending influence over the southeastern reaches of present-day Niger.
Hausa city-states such as Daura and later trading towns develop in the south, becoming hubs of agriculture and commerce.
Islam spreads among the ruling and merchant classes through trans-Saharan trade contacts.
Tuareg confederations found the sultanate of Agadez, which becomes a key caravan city on the trans-Saharan trade routes.
The Songhai Empire expands eastward, bringing parts of western Niger under its control.
Songhai ruler Askia Muhammad conquers Agadez, incorporating the Aïr region into the empire.
Moroccan forces defeat Songhai at the Battle of Tondibi, fragmenting power across the western Sahel.
The Kanem-Bornu Empire dominates the Lake Chad basin and the southeastern Niger lands at its height.
The Fulani jihad led by Usman dan Fodio establishes the Sokoto Caliphate, which absorbs Hausa territories in the south.
German explorer Heinrich Barth crosses the region, producing detailed accounts of Agadez and the Hausa lands for Europe.
An Anglo-French agreement begins delineating European spheres of influence across the central Sudan, foreshadowing colonial partition.
The brutal Voulet-Chanoine mission marches across the region, massacring villages as France pushes its conquest of the Sahel.
France establishes the Military Territory of Zinder, beginning formal colonial administration of Niger.
French forces complete the conquest of the Hausa south after suppressing local resistance.
The Tuareg revolt led by Kaocen besieges Agadez before being crushed by French colonial forces.
Niger becomes a fully constituted colony within French West Africa, with its capital later fixed at Niamey.
The colonial capital is moved from Zinder to Niamey, centering administration on the Niger River.
Niger gains representation in the French Union and political parties such as the PPN form as nationalist activity grows.
Niger votes in the French constitutional referendum and becomes an autonomous republic within the French Community.
Niger gains full independence from France on 3 August, with Hamani Diori as its first president.
Niger is admitted to the United Nations on 20 September.
A severe Sahelian drought begins, devastating Niger's agriculture and livestock through the early 1970s.
Lieutenant Colonel Seyni Kountché overthrows President Diori in a military coup amid famine and corruption charges.
Kountché dies in office and is succeeded by his chief of staff, Ali Saibou.
Pro-democracy protests and a renewed Tuareg rebellion in the north pressure the regime toward political reform.
A National Conference strips the president of most powers and sets the country on a path to multiparty democracy.
Mahamane Ousmane wins Niger's first free multiparty elections, becoming its first democratically elected president.
A peace accord ends the main Tuareg rebellion in the north.
Colonel Ibrahim Baré Maïnassara seizes power in a military coup and later wins a disputed election.
Baré Maïnassara is assassinated in a coup; Mamadou Tandja is elected president after a new constitution is adopted.
A locust invasion and drought trigger a major food crisis affecting millions of Nigeriens.
A second Tuareg rebellion erupts in the uranium-rich north under the MNJ movement.
President Tandja is ousted in a coup after he extends his rule, and a transitional government is installed.
Mahamadou Issoufou wins multiparty elections, restoring civilian rule.
Coordinated suicide bombings strike a military base and the Areva uranium mine at Arlit, signaling growing jihadist threats.
Niger joins the regional Multinational Joint Task Force to combat Boko Haram around Lake Chad.
Mohamed Bazoum is inaugurated after the first peaceful democratic transfer of power between elected presidents in Niger's history.
Members of the presidential guard detain President Bazoum and seize power in a military coup led by General Tchiani.
Niger orders the withdrawal of US and French forces and aligns with Mali and Burkina Faso in the Alliance of Sahel States.
